content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 10, 1990


L. G. Statham
1008 10th Street #514
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-246

Dear Ms. Statham:

You have requested advice on behalf of a nonprofit corporation to be formed. You wish to know if an elected officer can engage in certain activities with respect to the corporation consistent with the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Because your questions are general in nature and do not involve a specific decision to be made under the Act, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2

QUESTIONS

1. Can an elected officer solicit money on behalf of the corporation?

2. Can an elected officer donate his services as an announcer on behalf of the corporation?

CONCLUSIONS

1. The elected officer can solicit donations on behalf of the nonprofit corporation. The corporation, as described, is presumed not to be a controlled committee.

________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to the 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-90-246
Page 2


2. The elected officer can donate his services as an announcer on behalf of the nonprofit corporation. However, advertisements which feature the elected officer may be contributions to the elected officer by the nonprofit corporation.

FACTS

A nonprofit corporation is being formed as a tax-exempt organization under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code. The corporation is formed for educational purposes. It will not be run on a day to day basis by a candidate or elected officer. Its name will not incorporate the name of a candidate or elected officer. It will not engage in lobbying. It will not make contributions to candidates or to committees which support initiatives. The corporation will have three directors. One director will be the spouse of a candidate. The spouse will also be an employee of the corporation.

ANALYSIS

The involvement of an elected officer in the affairs of a nonprofit organization can result in the organization being treated as a controlled committee of the elected officer in certain circumstances. Because elected officers often are involved with nonprofit organizations, the Commission recently adopted a regulation that sets forth the circumstances in which a nonprofit organization is considered a controlled committee. (Regulation 18217, copy enclosed.)³

The regulation provides, in part, as follows:

(a) A nonprofit organization, as defined in subdivision (f), shall be considered a controlled committee, if both of the following apply:

(1) A candidate,⁴ his or her agent, or any committee he or she controls, exercises significant influence over the actions and decisions of the organization, or acts jointly with the organization in connection with the making of expenditures.


___________________________
3 Regulation 18217 was adopted by the Commission at its March 13, 1990 meeting. The regulation is currently in the rulemaking process. However, it represents the policy of the Commission, and we are issuing advice on the basis of the language of the regulation effective April 24, 1990.

4 An elected officer continues to be a candidate until his or her status as a candidate is terminated pursuant to Section 84214.

Our File No. I-90-246
Page 3


(2) The organization qualifies as a com-
mittee under Government Code Section 82013(a),
and the organization is operated for political
purposes. For purposes of this regulation, an
organization is "operated for political
purposes" if either of the following applies:

(A) The organization receives or
expends funds for the purpose of
influencing or attempting to influence
the action of the voters for or against
the nomination or election of a candidate
or the qualification or passage of any
measure.

(B) The organization makes
contributions to candidates or their
controlled committees.

* * *

(f) For purposes of this regulation,
"nonprofit organization" means an organization
which is characterized as an exempt organization
under Section 501 of the Internal Revenue Code, but
which is not an organization formed or existing
primarily for political purposes.

The regulation also provides guidance as to when a nonprofit
organization is presumed not to be a controlled committee
notwithstanding a candidate's involvement. A nonprofit organiza-
tion which is tax-exempt under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal
Revenue Code is presumed not to be controlled by a candidate if
(1) the candidate is not substantially involved in the day-to-day
operations of the organization; (2) the organization has a board
of directors with 3 or more members, two-thirds of whom are not
candidates, agents, staff or employees of candidates, or specified
relatives of a candidate who is a board member; and (3) the
candidate's name is not included in the name of the organization.
(Regulation 18217(b).)

Even if a candidate exercises control over a nonprofit
organization, it will not be considered a controlled committee
unless it is operated for political purposes. We presume that
most tax-exempt nonprofit organizations are not operated for
political purposes if they do not make contributions to
candidates, expend significant amounts to support or oppose ballot
measures or influence legislative action, or include the
candidate's name in the name of the organization. (Regulation
18217(c).)

According to the facts that you provided, we would presume
that the nonprofit corporation described in your letter would not
be a controlled committee. The corporation is to be tax-exempt

Our File No. I-90-246
Page 4

under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code. You state
that two-thirds of the board of directors will be "public"
members. By "public" we assume that you mean they will not be
agents, staff or relatives of candidates. A candidate's name is
not in the name of the corporation. The corporation will not make
contributions to candidates or spend funds to influence legisla-
tive action or to support or oppose ballot measures.

This presumption is not altered by the fact that the spouse
of the elected officer is employed by the organization. However,
if the spouse is acting as the agent of the elected officer, then
the elected officer may, in fact, be substantially involved in the
day to day activities of the corporation, so as to negate the
presumption. We have previously advised you that the employment
of the spouse may affect the ability of the elected officer to
make donations or loans to the nonprofit corporation with campaign
funds. (STATHAM Advice Letter No. I-89-690, copy enclosed.)

The mere fact that an elected officer solicits donations for
the organization does not mean that those donations are "contribu-
tions." A person who makes a donation to a nonprofit organization
which is tax-exempt under Section 501(c)(3) is not making a
political contribution unless the person knows or has reason to
know that the organization is operated for political purposes.
(Regulation 18217(e).)

An elected officer may donate his services as an announcer on
behalf of the corporation. However, under some circumstances this
could result in a contribution to the candidate by the nonprofit
corporation. For example, if the elected officer donated his
services as an announcer for the corporation in an election year,
and the corporation paid for an advertisement which featured the
elected officer, the cost of the advertisement could be considered
a contribution to the elected officer. (See, MCCARTHY Advice Let-
ter No. A-90-163, copy enclosed.)

I trust that this letter provides you with the information
that you need. If you have any further questions, please contact
me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

By: Margaret W. Ellison
Counsel, Legal Division

KED:MWE:aa

Enclosures
